digest.top_line: PEACE-2: whole-pelvis RT null in very high-risk N0M0 prostate (cPFS HR 0.81, p=0.088), diverging from POP-RT and questioning elective pelvic RT.
digest.tldr: Prostate and breast carried the day, both circling one question: when does adding RT volume or dose stop paying off? PEACE-2 found whole-pelvis RT null in very high-risk N0M0 (diverging from POP-RT); breast data (EORTC IM-MS 20yr OS wash, Dutch + IMPORT HIGH boost omission) keep narrowing where extra RT earns its toxicity.

sites.intro: De-escalation dominates: boost omission (Dutch cohort, IMPORT HIGH), hypofractionation (DBCG HYPO), and 15yr APBI maturity, set against EORTC IM-MS where nodal RT's breast-cancer-mortality gain is erased by late cardiac/lung death.

studies.name: OLIGOMA (ARO-2021-09)
studies.tldr: mPFS 35.8 vs 20.4 mo, HR 0.48 (0.25-0.91), p=0.021, adding ablative RT to all mets in oligometastatic breast.
details: 🔍 Phase 2 RCT: metastatic breast (any line), ≤5 mets; randomised systemic therapy ± ablative RT to ALL mets
🔍 Ablative RT delivered to every metastatic lesion; palliative RT to symptomatic mets allowed, but pts needing it to all mets excluded
📊 First RCT to show a PFS benefit from MDT in oligometastatic breast cancer (investigators' claim)
📊 Second co-primary, QoL (EORTC QLQ-C30) at 12 wks, met non-inferiority; MDT did not impair short-term QoL
🔍 Population: mostly ER/PR+ HER2-neg, first-line; >80% had 1-3 mets, ~2/3 of lesions bone (favorable ablation targets)
⚠️ Recruitment stopped early: <20% of initial planned N, <40% of amended target; final N=87 (43 vs 44)
⚠️ Underpowered: the PFS CI is wide and the upper bound approaches 1.0, so the estimate is fragile despite significance
⚠️ RT dose, fractionation, and technique not reported in source; transferability to practice can't be judged
🔗 Adds breast-specific RCT support to MDT signals (e.g. SABR-COMET); confirmatory trials ongoing (TAORMINA, STEREO-SEIN, LARA, others)

verdict.rationale: Randomised but recruitment stopped at <20% of planned (<40% of amended) accrual; N=87 underpowered, estimate fragile. Numerous confirmatory MDT trials still ongoing.
verdict.audience: Oligometastatic breast (≤5 mets), mostly ER+/HER2-neg, first-line systemic

studies.significance: The subtle co-primary is QoL: EORTC QLQ-C30 was non-inferior at 12 wks, so ablative RT to all mets bought the PFS gain without degrading short-term QoL, the read that de-risks offering MDT. Population skewed bone-met-dominant, ER+/HER2-neg, first-line, so it transfers best to favorable oligomet targets. Dose and fractionation not reported in source.
open_questions: Which oligometastatic breast subgroups benefit most from MDT
OS benefit from MDT beyond the PFS signal
Optimal RT dose and fractionation for ablative MDT in breast

studies.name: APBI-IMRT Florence
studies.tldr: 15yr IBTR 7.7% vs 4.2% (APBI vs WBI), HR 1.57 (0.82-3.04) p=0.17; excess from new primaries not true relapse, OS equivalent.
details: 📊 IBTR excess is new ipsilateral primaries, not tumor-bed relapse; true local relapse essentially equal between arms
🔍 Phase III equivalence trial, N=520, 1:1 randomized 2005-2013, post-BCS, pT<25mm, margins ≥5mm, age >40
🔍 APBI 30Gy/5fx IMRT vs WBI 50Gy/25fx + 10Gy/5fx tumor-bed boost
⚠️ Absolute IBTR gap is larger at 15yr than at 5 or 10yr; de-escalation risk only fully visible with mature f/u
⚠️ Powered for 5yr IBTR equivalence (Δ5%, 80% power); the 15yr between-arm comparison is descriptive, not a powered test
⚠️ Highly selected low-risk pts; result doesn't transfer to younger (<40), larger, or margin-positive disease
🔗 Extends Florence IMRT-APBI (Livi EJC 2015, Meattini JCO 2020) to 15yr; complements EBRT-APBI RCTs IMPORT-LOW, RAPID, NSABP B-39

figures.caption: APBI 30Gy/5fx IMRT vs WBI 50Gy/25fx + 10Gy/5fx boost; N=520, 1:1, 2005-2013

verdict.rationale: Phase III RCT with mature 15yr data; aligns with guideline-recognized partial-breast de-escalation, so reinforces existing practice rather than changing it.
verdict.audience: Early breast cancer, pT<25mm, margins ≥5mm, age >40, post-lumpectomy

studies.significance: The reassuring RT read: true local relapse was near-identical (2.1 vs 1.6%), so the higher IBTR is new ipsilateral primaries elsewhere in the breast, not tumor-bed failure. 30Gy/5fx IMRT-APBI holds local control at 15yr, supporting partial-breast as the default offer for pT<25mm, margin-negative, >40yr pts.

studies.name: IMPORT HIGH
studies.tldr: 10yr IBTR 3.7% with 48Gy/15F SIB (3wk) vs 3.5% sequential 40+16Gy boost; 53Gy escalation worse (5.5%), reaffirming 48Gy SIB as SOC.
details: 🔍 Phase 3 RCT, 1:1:1, N=2617, 76 UK hospitals (2009-2015); pT1-3 pN0-3a M0 post-BCS, all needing tumour bed boost
🔍 SIB (48 & 53Gy arms) dose-paints the boost within the 15-fraction/3-week course; control adds a separate 16Gy/8F boost (40Gy/15F + 16Gy/8F)

details.text: 📊 Prior 5yr IBTR by arm (Lancet 2023; all arms low)

details: 📊 53Gy escalation arm showed numerically higher relapse with no OS or toxicity gain; further boost escalation not advantageous

details.text: 📊 10yr OS: no difference vs sequential boost (absolute % difference)
📊 Moderate/marked late AEs at 10yr (similar across all 3 arms)

details: 🔗 Boost value in higher-risk pts established by EORTC 22881-10882; IMPORT HIGH addresses delivery (hypofractionated SIB vs sequential), not whether to boost
📊 Both 40+16Gy and 48Gy arms stayed below the 5% relapse estimate assumed for the control in the original 5yr powering

verdict.rationale: 10yr update of a phase 3 RCT; reaffirms 48Gy/15F SIB as standard and shows further escalation gains nothing. Confirms the prior 5yr Lancet 2023 result.
verdict.audience: Early breast cancer (pT1-3 pN0-3a M0) post-BCS needing tumour bed boost

studies.significance: The deliverable change is the boost: 48Gy/15F SIB in 3 weeks holds non-inferior to a separate 40+16Gy sequential boost at 10yr, so the extra 8-fraction boost phase is droppable. Escalating to 53Gy raised relapse with no OS or late-toxicity gain, so don't dose-escalate beyond 48Gy SIB.
open_questions: Generalizability of hypofractionated SIB boost to nodal or partial-breast RT
Cosmetic/PRO durability beyond 5yr (photos and PROs collected only to 5 years)

studies.name: EORTC IM-MS Trial (22922/10925)
studies.tldr: OS identical at 20yr (61.0% vs 61.8%, HR 1.00, p=0.967): IM-MS RT's breast-cancer-mortality gain (HR 0.82) erased by excess cardiac/lung death (HR 1.26).
details: 🔍 Phase 3 RCT, N=2002/arm: IM-MS nodal RT (internal mammary + medial supraclavicular) vs none, stage I-III breast, 20-yr update
🔗 Earlier EORTC report showed DFS/DMFS/BCM gains; at 20yr only the BCM reduction endures (HR 0.82, unchanged)
📊 pN0 subgroup (20yr): no benefit from IM-MS RT
🔗 vs DBCG IMN2 (Nielsen 2024): modern IM-RT mean heart dose 4-9x lower (MHD 1.2 Gy right, 2.3 Gy left)
🔗 DBCG IMN2: modern IM-RT cut distant mets + BCM and improved OS in node-positive pts at 15yr
⚠️ 20yr OS wash = excess non-BCM (cardiac/lung) death offsetting a persistent BCM benefit, not lost tumor control
⚠️ Net benefit is technique-dependent: modern cardiac-sparing RT may preserve the BCM gain without the late mortality penalty
⚠️ pN0 pts derive no benefit → supports omitting IM-MS RT in node-negative disease

verdict.rationale: Definitive 20-yr RCT update; BCM benefit (HR 0.82) persists and the OS wash is explained by older-era cardiac/lung dose, consistent with modern cardiac-sparing, node-positive-selective practice.
verdict.audience: Stage I-III breast cancer, central/medial tumor or node-positive

studies.significance: The flat 20-yr OS reflects a tradeoff, not RT failure: IM-MS RT cut breast-cancer mortality (HR 0.82), but excess non-breast-cancer death (HR 1.26, cardiac/lung) erased it. Trial-era heart dose ran 4-9x DBCG IMN2's; modern cardiac-sparing is what makes IM-MS coverage worth it. pN0 pts gained nothing.
open_questions: Whether modern cardiac-sparing technique preserves the BCM benefit without excess non-BCM mortality
Whether IM-MS nodal coverage should be omitted given no 20-year survival gain

studies.name: DBCG HYPO
studies.tldr: 10-yr grade 2-3 induration 19.5% (40Gy/15fx) vs 24.7% (50Gy/25fx), HR 0.76; recurrence and OS non-inferior.
details: 🔍 Phase III non-inferiority RCT (1:1), Denmark/Norway/Germany 2009-2014, N=1,882, node-negative breast cancer or DCIS
🔍 1° EP: 3-yr grade ≥2 breast induration; reported here are the prespecified 10-yr toxicity, recurrence, and survival analyses
📊 No significant difference in locoregional recurrence, distant failure, or breast cancer mortality between arms
🔗 vs START-B (10-yr): same 40/15 vs 50/25 question; DBCG HYPO replicates non-inferior control with reduced late fibrosis
⚠️ 1° EP is a fibrosis/cosmesis surrogate, not an oncologic endpoint; trial powered for non-inferiority
⚠️ Node-negative/DCIS, whole-breast only; doesn't address hypofractionation with regional nodal irradiation

verdict.rationale: Phase III non-inferiority RCT, 12.8-yr f/u, confirming a modality already standard worldwide; reinforces 40/15 rather than changing practice. Consistent with START-B.
verdict.audience: Node-negative early breast cancer or DCIS, adjuvant whole-breast RT

studies.significance: The 10-yr induration HR 0.76 (0.62-0.92) means 40Gy/15fx is superior for late fibrosis, not merely non-inferior, with locoregional control and OS unchanged. The 12.8-yr median f/u closes the long-term-toxicity gap that kept some clinicians on 50Gy/25fx for node-negative whole-breast RT.

studies.name: DBCG RT Natural
studies.tldr: Invasive LR 1.5% with PBI (40Gy/15fr) vs 9.8% omitting it in low-risk ≥60yr breast; RT+ET gave 0 recurrences.
details: 🔍 ≥60yr, pT1N0, ER≥10%, HER2-normal, grade 1-2, unifocal non-lobular, post-lumpectomy, margin ≥2mm
🔍 Randomised PBI 40Gy/15fr vs no PBI; stratified by institution + ET use; plus a self-selecting no-PBI cohort
📊 1° EP 5-yr invasive LR; prespecified expected 2%, max acceptable 4%, omission arm blew past it
📊 39 of 41 local recurrences were in pts who had no PBI; all 41 invasive, 36 isolated
⚠️ ET not randomised: -ET pooled 'ET not indicated (low-risk)' with pts on ET <4.5y, so RT×ET cells aren't a clean factorial
⚠️ Median FU 4yr for a 5-yr 1° EP; reported early on planned stopping rules, absolute LR may climb with maturity
🔗 Contests the RT-omission trend (PRIME II, LUMINA): those accepted omitting RT in low-risk elderly on ET; here omission carried clear LR cost
🔗 vs EUROPA: in elderly low-risk, RT showed better QoL than endocrine therapy (curator framing), strengthening the case to treat over omit

verdict.rationale: Randomised phase III contests the RT-omission trend (PRIME II/LUMINA) for low-risk elderly; divergence robust (DMC-stopped), though reported early at 4yr median FU vs 5yr 1° EP.
verdict.audience: Low-risk early breast, ≥60yr, pT1N0 ER+ HER2-normal grade 1-2, post-lumpectomy

studies.significance: Either modality alone stayed under the 4% threshold (-RT+ET 3.7%, +RT-ET 3.0%), but RT is the adherence-proof one: the -ET group pools 'ET not indicated' with sub-4.5y dropouts, so omission's recurrence cost partly reflects lost endocrine therapy. With 5-fraction PBI, treating low-risk elderly is easier to justify than omitting.
open_questions: Whether RT can be safely omitted in pts with good documented ET adherence
Optimal PBI fractionation (15fr vs 5fr) in this low-risk population
Whether absolute LR rates rise with longer follow-up

studies.name: HypoG-01
studies.tldr: LRR rare (20/118 first events) and mostly in-volume (67%); failure patterns comparable between 40Gy/15fx and 50Gy/25fx, validating ESTRO nodal contouring.
details: 🔍 Pre-planned secondary (patterns-of-failure + dosimetric) analysis, ITT, of HypoG-01 phase III: 40 Gy/15 fx (3wk) vs 50 Gy/25 fx (5wk), all + tumour-bed boost; N=1,260, median f/u 4.8y
📊 LRR was the lowest-frequency first event: 20/118; distant recurrence and second malignancy dominated
📐 iLRR sites mostly in-volume: 20/30 within CTV (67%) → ESTRO-guided contouring covered where recurrences arose
📐 iLRR nodal sites: 19/30, mainly levels I & II (axillary)
🔗 Patterns of failure not obviously different between 3-wk and 5-wk arms → moderate hypofractionation does not shift the failure pattern
⚠️ Descriptive analysis; only ~20 LRR events split across two arms — underpowered to detect a between-arm difference, so 'no difference' is not equivalence
⚠️ Competing risks dominate: distant recurrence and second malignancy were the main first events; locoregional control is not the limiting factor in this population

figures.caption: First events (N=118): isolated distant recurrence n=61, second malignancy n=37, isolated LRR n=19, concomitant LRR n=1.

verdict.rationale: Pre-planned secondary analysis of a mature phase III RCT; descriptive patterns support moderate hypofractionation and ESTRO contouring. Few LRR events limit between-arm inference.
verdict.audience: Breast cancer, adjuvant locoregional RT including regional nodal irradiation

studies.significance: The actionable read is target-volume validation: 20/30 isolated-LRR sites (67%) fell within the CTV and 19/30 were nodal (mainly axillary I-II), so ESTRO-guided contouring is covering where these cancers recur. Failure patterns held across both fractionation arms, supporting 40Gy/15fx with nodal coverage without re-drawing volumes.
open_questions: Long-term LRR and second-malignancy rates beyond 4.8y follow-up
Whether nodal CTV can be tailored given level I-II failure predominance

studies.name: Dutch Breast Boost Indication Study
studies.tldr: 10y IBTR 1.2% with or without boost in 0-2 risk-factor pts → tumour-bed boost omittable in the modern systemic-therapy era.
details: 🔍 Retrospective Dutch national cohort, breast-conserving treatment 2012-2016, boost vs no boost
🔍 1° outcome: ipsilateral breast tumour recurrence (IBTR), found via pathology-report algorithm
🔗 Boost cuts IBTR ~50% in EORTC 22881/10882 (Bartelink, Lancet Oncol 2015), the basis for the current boost indication
📐 Assisi omission thresholds: 10y IBTR <3% (boost pts) or <6% (no-boost pts)
📊 Thresholds exceeded only in the ≥3-risk-factor boost arm at 10y (3.3%); all other subgroups well under
⚠️ Boost non-randomized → confounding by indication (higher-risk pts were selected for boost)
⚠️ ≥3-risk-factor no-boost arm tiny (N=149) → unstable 10y estimate

subdetails: Guideline-indicated systemic therapy not adequately given
No pCR after neoadjuvant chemo in TNBC/HER2+

verdict.rationale: Retrospective national-registry cohort; boost non-randomized (confounding by indication). Low absolute IBTR backs emerging boost-omission in low-risk pts; ≥3-RF no-boost arm too small (N=149) to settle that group.
verdict.audience: Early breast cancer, breast-conserving surgery + whole-breast RT

studies.significance: The omission decision turns on absolute IBTR, not the boost-vs-no-boost contrast (confounded by indication: boost arms run higher because higher-risk pts got it). At 0-2 risk factors 10y IBTR is 1.2% either way, so the tumour-bed boost is omittable; ≥3 factors stays open.
open_questions: Does a tumour-bed boost help pts with ≥3 risk factors?
Which subgroups still benefit from a tumour-bed boost?

details: 🔍 Phase III open-label non-inferiority, N≈434, randomized 1:1 stratified by risk group + nodal status (Tata Memorial Centre)
🔍 Eligibility: high-risk, very-high-risk and/or node-positive non-metastatic prostate cancer; ECOG 0-2; PSMA PET/CT staging allowed

details.text: 📊 Dose / fractionation by arm (both deliver whole-pelvis RT)

details: 📊 1° EP BFFS (Phoenix nadir+2): interim 1-2y shows no inferiority signal for SBRT; mature 4-5y data pending
📊 MFS and OS: no difference in early analysis, both immature

details.text: 📊 Toxicity (Grade ≥2) by arm, interim — all differences NS

details: ⚠️ Interim safety/early-efficacy only; non-inferiority NOT established with 1-2y f/u and immature BFFS curves
🔗 vs HYPO-RT-PC: PRIME extends 5fx ultra-hypofx to node+ disease with whole-pelvis RT + ADT; HYPO-RT-PC was node-neg, prostate-only, no ADT

figures.caption: HYPO-RT-PC 10y FFS 72% vs 65%; adjusted HR 0.84 (95% CI 0.69-1.03)

verdict.rationale: Interim 1-2y analysis; primary endpoint BFFS immature (mature 4-5y pending), so non-inferiority unestablished. Interim readouts cap at early-signal regardless of clean phase III design.
verdict.audience: High-risk or node-positive non-metastatic prostate cancer, long-course ADT

studies.significance: The novel move is whole-pelvis nodal RT compressed to 5 fractions (25 Gy/5fx, SIB to involved nodes) in node+/high-risk disease, where HYPO-RT-PC stopped at prostate-only node-negative. Interim grade 3+ GU/GI <1% is the reassuring safety read; BFFS is too immature to commit to nodal SBRT yet.

studies.name: PIVOTALboost
studies.tldr: Late G2+ bowel 6.5% with pelvic-node RT + focal boost vs 8.2% prostate-only (20fx IMRT); early bowel excess resolved by 18wk.

details.text: 🔍 Phase 3 RCT, N=1465 randomised at 39 UK centres; 3 arms, all 20-fraction IMRT
subdetails: PPN+B: + pelvic-node IMRT + focal boost (462 received)

details: 🔍 20-fraction moderately hypofractionated IMRT with focal boost to the dominant intraprostatic lesion; dose in Gy not reported in source
📊 Pelvic-node RT raised early (<18wk) bowel side effects; the excess resolved by 18 weeks post-RT
📊 Late (2yr) G2+ bowel and bladder not increased by nodal RT or boost; PPN+B arm numerically lowest of the three
⚠️ Toxicity-only secondary analysis; primary endpoint (biochemical/clinical failure) not reported here, so efficacy of boost + nodal RT stays unproven
⚠️ 'Preliminary' late data: only 973/1314 (74%) have ≥2yr follow-up; late toxicity could still rise with maturity
🔗 vs FLAME (JCO 2021): focal boost improved biochemical control without added late toxicity in conventional fractionation; PIVOTALboost tests the same boost concept in 20fx
🔗 vs POP-RT (JCO 2021): prophylactic whole-pelvis RT improved bPFS in high-risk prostate; here the added pelvic RT looks tolerable at 2yr

verdict.rationale: Preliminary toxicity-only secondary readout; primary biochemical/clinical-failure endpoint not yet reported and only 74% have 2-yr follow-up. Safety reassuring but efficacy and mature late toxicity pending.

studies.significance: Reassurance sits in the nodal arm: whole-pelvis RT + focal boost in 20fx gave late G2+ bowel 6.5%, bladder 16.5%, the lowest of three arms, not a penalty. The early nodal-RT bowel excess resolved by 18wk. Toxicity no longer gates adding elective nodal coverage; the biochemical-control primary endpoint is still pending.
open_questions: Does focal boost + pelvic-node RT improve biochemical control in 20-fraction prostate RT?
Durability of late GU/GI toxicity beyond 2 years

studies.name: PACE-NODES
studies.tldr: Acute GI CTCAE ≥2 28% vs 21% adding pelvic-nodal vs prostate-only 5fx SBRT, transient; no GU difference; efficacy not yet mature.
details: 🔍 Phase 3 RCT, 1166 randomised 1:1 (target 1128), UK multicentre
🔍 Eligibility: high-risk localised (T3a-T4, Gleason 8-10, or PSA>20), planned 12-36mo ADT
⚠️ GI excess was transient: resolved by 12wk, no between-arm difference at that timepoint
📊 EPIC-26 bowel domain worse with PPN at 4wk, PRO mirrors the clinician GI signal
📊 No difference in acute GU toxicity, clinician- or patient-reported
⚠️ Feasibility gap: 11% PPN-SBRT vs 4% P-SBRT unallocated, mostly unmet dose constraints
⚠️ Acute-toxicity readout only; 1° efficacy endpoint (time to biochemical/clinical failure) not yet mature
🔗 Nodal RT benefit shown with moderate hypofractionation (POP-RT); PACE-NODES tests whether 5fx nodal SBRT delivers it as safely

verdict.rationale: Acute-toxicity readout only; 1° efficacy endpoint (time to biochemical/clinical failure) explicitly not yet mature. Randomised phase 3, but no cancer-control data to change practice.
verdict.audience: High-risk localised prostate (T3a-T4, Gleason 8-10, or PSA>20), 12-36mo ADT

studies.significance: The deliverability signal gates adoption: 11% of PPN-SBRT pts didn't receive their allocation (vs 4% prostate-only), mostly unmet dose constraints, so 5fx whole-pelvis SBRT isn't a drop-in for every high-risk case. The GI excess (28% vs 21%) was transient and GU was untouched, but cancer-control data remain pending before adding nodal coverage at this fractionation.
open_questions: Does adding pelvic nodal SBRT improve biochemical/clinical failure?
Late GI/GU toxicity of 5-fraction nodal SBRT
Nodal dose constraints driving unmet allocation in PPN arm

studies.name: PEACE-2
studies.tldr: Whole-pelvis RT null vs prostate-only in very high-risk N0M0: cPFS HR 0.81 (p=0.088), bPFS HR 0.97, MFS HR 0.93, no added toxicity.
details: 🔍 Phase III RCT, 2×2 factorial (±cabazitaxel × prostate-only vs pelvic RT); this readout = the pelvic-RT axis only, cabazitaxel axis not reported in source
🔍 Eligibility: very high-risk localized N0M0, ≥2 of Gleason ≥8 / T3-T4 / PSA ≥20; staged by conventional imaging or choline PET/CT
🔍 RT: dose-escalated IMRT, whole pelvis + prostate boost (prostate 78 Gy EQD2) vs prostate-only IMRT
💊 3yr ADT (GnRH analog + antiandrogen) across all arms
📊 No additional toxicity with whole-pelvis RT; comparable grade ≥2 GU between arms

details.text: 🔗 Why PEACE-2 diverges from POP-RT (where WPRT was significantly positive)
subdetails: Higher dose escalation: 78 Gy EQD2 to prostate
Contemporary systemic intensification + modern imaging in the PEACE-2 era

details: ⚠️ Source labels this an interim analysis (~5.5yr median f/u); cPFS p=0.088 is borderline and could shift with longer follow-up
⚠️ Investigators: <1 in 10 men died of prostate cancer in decade 1, challenging the 'very high-risk' label, esp. with negative modern imaging

figures.caption: PEACE-2 schema: four arms, ±cabazitaxel crossed with prostate-only vs pelvic RT; primary endpoint cPFS

verdict.rationale: Randomised phase III; pelvic RT null where POP-RT was strongly positive, so the field is now contested. Interim ~5.5yr f/u and borderline cPFS temper a firm reversal.
verdict.audience: Very high-risk localized prostate (N0M0), ≥2 of Gleason ≥8/T3-T4/PSA ≥20

studies.significance: The WPRT decision: PEACE-2 is null on cPFS (67.1% vs 62.9% at 7y, HR 0.81, p=0.088), bPFS and MFS, where POP-RT was strongly positive. With 3yr ADT, dose-escalated IMRT and modern staging, the incremental value of elective pelvic nodal RT looks small and non-significant, reopening whether routine whole-pelvis RT is needed in N0M0 very-high-risk disease.
open_questions: Does longer follow-up change the borderline cPFS signal?
Biomarkers to select very-high-risk men benefiting from pelvic nodal RT
Does the cabazitaxel axis alter the pelvic RT benefit?

studies.name: OligoCare
studies.tldr: Local in-field failure 5.0% at 1yr, 11.4% at 3yr (LC 88.6%) across 2447 SABR-treated oligomet pts; CRC worst.

details.text: 🔍 Prospective EORTC real-world SABR registry (OligoCare), interim analysis
subdetails: 57 institutions, 2447 eligible pts / 3533 lesions; enrolled Jul 2019-Jul 2025

details: 📐 Minimum PTV dose was the most critical technical factor; correlated with local control (dose-response magnitude not reported in source)
🔗 De novo OMD had better local control than repeat OMD, attributed to higher delivered dose (comparison magnitude not reported)
⚠️ CRC had the worst local control despite the highest median dose/fraction, flagging relative radioresistance and a need for dose-escalation/combination
⚠️ Only local in-field progression reported; no distant-failure, PFS, or OS, so this isn't a survival signal
⚠️ Real-world registry with no randomised comparator and no metastasis-directed vs systemic-only contrast; selection bias toward fitter pts likely
🔗 vs SABR-COMET (Lancet 2019): that randomised trial showed an OS signal; OligoCare adds real-world LC, no survival endpoint

figures.caption: Local in-field progression: 5.0% at 1yr, 11.4% at 3yr; 237 events / 2447 pts.

verdict.rationale: Real-world SABR registry, single cohort, no randomised comparator, explicitly an interim analysis; descriptive local control only despite mature 31-mo follow-up.
verdict.audience: Oligometastatic solid tumors, de novo or repeat OMD

studies.significance: The RT-actionable lever is minimum PTV dose, named the single most critical technical factor; de novo mets beat repeat OMD, attributed to higher delivered dose. Colorectal failed most (3yr 19.6%) despite the highest median dose/fraction, so escalate or combine for CRC rather than rely on standard SABR dosing.
open_questions: Does SABR local control translate to OS or PFS benefit in oligomets?
Optimal dose escalation or combination strategy for colorectal metastases?
Minimum PTV dose threshold maximizing local control?

studies.name: DIREKHT
studies.tldr: Primary CTV de-escalated to 56 Gy and/or contralateral neck spared in resected HNSCC; no outcome data reported in source.
details: 🔍 Post-operative RT de-intensification in resected HNSCC
🔍 Lever 1: primary CTV dose reduced to 56 Gy
🔍 Lever 2: contralateral neck spared in a specified pt subgroup
🔗 56 Gy primary CTV sits below conventional post-op dose — a deliberate de-escalation
⚠️ Source is a framing tweet on trial design; no 1° endpoint, effect size, or CI reported
⚠️ No locoregional control, survival, or toxicity readout in source
⚠️ Contralateral neck sparing criteria undefined in source — applicability gate unknown

verdict.rationale: Source is a framing tweet describing the de-escalation design only; no primary endpoint, effect size, comparator, or follow-up reported to classify.

studies.significance: Two RT levers are explicit: primary CTV cut to 56 Gy and contralateral neck omitted in selected pts, both aimed at reducing late toxicity (xerostomia, dysphagia, neck morbidity) in resected HNSCC. This is the post-op dose de-escalation plus elective contralateral-nodal-omission decision, though the source gives the design without a locoregional-control or toxicity readout to act on.

(MFS) and Eugonadal MFS in the RADIOSA phase
Il randomized trial
15-19 MAY 2026

ESTRO
2026
0000g
Background
Metastasis-free survival (MFS) has been validated as a surrogate endpoint for overall survival in non-metastatic prostate
cancer, and may represent an adaptable and clinically meaningful endpoint in oligorecurrent disease.
The phase II RADIOSA trial compared stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT) alone (Arm A) versus SBRT plus 6-month
androgen deprivation therapy (ADT, Arm B). This post-hoe analysis evaluated MFS and eugonadal MFS after
testosterone recovery, with a visual mapping of disease trajectories through a Sankey diagram.
Methods
A total of 102 patients were randomized 1:l to Arm A (SBRT) or Arm B (SBRT + ADT).
Median follow-up (Reverse KM) was 49.23 months (95% C1 42.47-54.8).
MFS was defined as the time between randomisation and the appearance of a metastatic recurrence (any MI) on imaging.
Eugonadal MFS was calculated from testosterone recovery to new metastasis or last follow-up.
Kaplan-Meier (KM) survival curves were compared with log-rank tests; hazard ratios were estimated using Cox models.

(62.7%) patients in Arm A and 19/51 (37.3%) in Arm B.
Median MFS was 16.6 months (95% CI 12.83-NA) in
Arm A and not reached in Arm B, with a 61% risk
reduction for metastatic progression (log-rank test, p -

All patients enrolled in arm B, except two, reached
testosterone recovery within the follow-up period. After
testosterone recovery, eugonadal MFS
remained
significantly longer in Arm B (Figure 1b, p < 0.05).
confirming that the advantage of the combination persisted
beyond
pharmacologic
castration.

Conclusions
• Low IBTR rates across all patient subgroups
• Based on the presence of combined risk factors, 5-and 10-year IBTR rates exceeded
the Assisi thresholds only in:
• Patients with ≥ 3 risk factors treated with a boost at 10 years
Take home messages
• The low IBTR rates across all subgroups suggest that
• In patients with 0-2 risk factors a tumour bed boost can be omitted, in the modern
systemic therapy era
• In patients with ≥ 3 risk factors the clinical relevance of a tumour bed boost remains
open to discussion
• Further research is needed to identify specific subgroups of patients who benefit from a
boost

Background & Methods
Background
Moderately hypofractionated adjuvant whole-breast radiotherapy for early
node-negative breast cancer is standard worldwide, but long-term
randomised evidence remains limited. Here, we present the Danish
Breast Cancer Group HYPO trial prespecified 10-year outcomes.
Methods
• Phase III randomised non-inferiority trial (1:1) across Denmark,
Norway and Germany (2009-2014)
• 1,882 women with node-negative breast cancer or DCIS
• Whole-breast RT: 50 Gy/25 fractions vs 40 Gy/15 fractions
• Primary endpoint: 3-year grade 22 breast induration at two consequtive
visits, or at final follow-up
• Morbidity evaluated year 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 10
• These results are the prespecified 10-year analyses of toxicity.

Conclusion & Take-Home Message
40 Gy / 15 fractions reduced long-term breast
induration without compromising recurrence or
survival outcomes.
At 12.8 years follow-up, results showed:
• Lower fibrosis risk with maintained cancer outcomes
• Supports continued use of moderately hypofractionated
whole-breast radiotherapy
Less toxicity. Same cancer
-control.

Discussion
• OLIGOMA is the first RCT to demonstrate a PFS-benefit from MDT in
patients with oligometastatic breast cancer.
• Majority had ER/PR pos. HER2 neg. disease in the first line-setting
• >80% of patients had 1-3 metastases, 2/3 of lesions were bone metastases.
• Short-term quality of life was not impaired by MDT.
• Recruitment was stopped early (< 20% of initial, < 40% of amended #)
• Numerous trials are still ongoing (incl. TAORMINA, STEREO-SEIN, LARA,
CLEAR, COSMO, ARCHER, ISTMET, OLIGAMI).
• Comprehensive analyses are needed to determine which patients benefit
most.

2026
At 15 years median of follow up, the phase III APBI-IMRT Florence trial (NCT02104895)
demonstrates durable oncological outcomes with PB in selected patients with early breast cancer
Although a numerical increase in IBTR was observed, this was largely attributable to new
primary tumours rather than true local recurrences.
No differences were observed in loco-regional control, distant metastasis, breast cancer-
specific survival or overall survival.
These findings provide mature evidence supporting PBl as a safe de-escalation strategy and
reinforce its role as a standard treatment option in appropriately selected patients

• POP-RT (2 years ADT) demonstrated significant benefit in bFFS, cFFS, and MFS with WPRT.
• PEACE-2 (3 years ADT, modern imaging, dose-escalated RT) showed no significant improvement in bPFS, MFS, CSS, or OS.
